subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A simple pendulum of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> has a wooden bob of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>. It is struck by a bullet of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> moving with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The bullet gets embedded into the bob. The height to which the bob rises before swinging back is. (use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A simple pendulum of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> has a wooden bob of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>. It is struck by a bullet of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> moving with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The bullet gets embedded into the bob. The height to which the bob rises before swinging back is. (use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Linear momentum will be conserved, therefore

mu=M+mV

⇒10−2×2×102≅1×V

⇒V≅2 m s-1

Again from energy conservation, we get height achieved, h=V22g=0.2m.

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A proton of mass $\mathrm{m}$ collides elastically with a particle of unknown mass at rest. After the collision, the proton and the unknown particle are seen moving at an angle of $90^{\circ}$ with respect to each other. The mass of unknown particle is:
question: A proton of mass $\mathrm{m}$ collides elastically with a particle of unknown mass at rest. After the collision, the proton and the unknown particle are seen moving at an angle of $90^{\circ}$ with respect to each other. The mass of unknown particle is:
solution:
Apply principle of conservation of momentum along $x$-direction, $$ \begin{aligned} m u &=m v_1 \cos 45^{\circ}+M v_2 \cos 45^{\circ} \\ m u &=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left(m v_1+M v_2\right) \end{aligned} $$ Along $y$-direction, $$ \begin{aligned} &o=m v_1 \sin 45^{\circ}-M v_2 \sin 45^{\circ} \\ &o=\left(m v_1-M v_2\right) \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{aligned} $$
Coefficient of restution $e=1$ $$ =\frac{v_2-v_1 \cos 90}{u \cos 45} $$ ( $\because$ Collision is elastic) $$ \begin{aligned} &\Rightarrow \frac{v_2}{\frac{u}{\sqrt{2}}}=1 \\ &\Rightarrow u=\sqrt{2} v_2 \end{aligned} $$ Solving eqs (i), (ii), \& (iii), we get mass of unknown particle, $M=m$

q: Two identical particles move towards each other with velocity $2 v$ and $v$ respectively. The velocity of centre of mass is
question: Two identical particles move towards each other with velocity $2 v$ and $v$ respectively. The velocity of centre of mass is
solution:
Conserving Linear Momentum
$$
2 \mathrm{Mv}_{\mathrm{c}}=2 \mathrm{Mv}-\mathrm{Mv} \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{c}}=\mathrm{v} / 2
$$

The energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> after the collision is written as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>. The value of <math><mi>x</mi></math> is
question: A body <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0.1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> has an initial velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. After the collision, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> moves with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>v</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> after the collision is written as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>. The value of <math><mi>x</mi></math> is

0.1×3i^ + 0.1×5j^ = 0.1×4i^+j^ + 0.1×v→

v→=-i^ + j^ m s-1

v=v→=2 m s-1

KB=12×0.1×v2=110 J

x=1

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Three bodies A, B and C have equal kinetic energies and their masses are $400 \mathrm{~g}$. $1.2 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $1.6 \mathrm{~kg}$ respectively. The ratio of their linear momenta is :
question: Three bodies A, B and C have equal kinetic energies and their masses are $400 \mathrm{~g}$. $1.2 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $1.6 \mathrm{~kg}$ respectively. The ratio of their linear momenta is :
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{KE}=\frac{\mathrm{P}^2}{2 \mathrm{~m}} \\ & \mathrm{P} \propto \sqrt{\mathrm{m}}\end{aligned}$
Hence, $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}: \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}: \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{C}}$
$=\sqrt{400}: \sqrt{1200}: \sqrt{1600}=1: \sqrt{3}: 2$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A uniform thin metal plate of mass $10 \mathrm{~kg}$ with dimensions is shown. The ratio of $\mathrm{x}$ and $\mathrm{y}$ coordinates of center of mass of plate in $\frac{n}{9}$. The value of $n$ is ________
question: A uniform thin metal plate of mass $10 \mathrm{~kg}$ with dimensions is shown. The ratio of $\mathrm{x}$ and $\mathrm{y}$ coordinates of center of mass of plate in $\frac{n}{9}$. The value of $n$ is ________
solution:
$\mathrm{m}_1=\sigma \times 5=10 \mathrm{Kg}$

$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{x}_1+\mathrm{m}_2 \mathrm{x}_2=\mathrm{m}_3 \mathrm{x}_3 \\ & 10 \mathrm{x}_1+2(1.5)=12(1.5) \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}_1=1.5 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{y}_1+\mathrm{m}_2 \mathrm{y}_2=\mathrm{m}_3 \mathrm{y}_3 \\ & 10 \mathrm{y}_1+2(1.5)=12 \times 1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{y}_1=0.9 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{x}_1}{\mathrm{y}_1}=\frac{1.5}{0.9}=\frac{15}{9} \\ & \mathrm{n}=15\end{aligned}$

options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> right and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2.0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> above <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> mass.

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1.5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> right and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1.2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> above <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> mass.

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2.0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> right and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> above <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> mass.

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> right and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2.0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> above <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> mass.
solution:
Take 1 kg mass at origin

Xcm=1×0+1.5×3+2.5×05=0.9 cm
Ycm=1×0+1.5×0+2.5×45=2 cm

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: As per the given figure, a small ball P slides down the quadrant of a circle and hits the other ball Q of equal mass which is initially at rest. Neglecting the effect of friction and assume the collision to be elastic, the velocity of ball Q after collision will be : (g = 10 m s-2)
question: As per the given figure, a small ball P slides down the quadrant of a circle and hits the other ball Q of equal mass which is initially at rest. Neglecting the effect of friction and assume the collision to be elastic, the velocity of ball Q after collision will be : (g = 10 m s-2)
solution:
Let vp be the velocity of ball P just before collision. Therefore, applying conservation of energy for P (between the moment it is released and the moment just before collision),

mgl+0=12mvp2

⇒vp=2gl

          =2×10×0.2

         =2 m s-1 

Since both balls have equal masses and the collision is perfectly elastic, the velocities of both the balls will get interchanged.

Therefore, the velocity of ball Q just after the collision is 2 m s-1.

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: When kinetic energy of a body becomes 36 times of its original value, the percentage increase in the momentum of the body will be:
question: When kinetic energy of a body becomes 36 times of its original value, the percentage increase in the momentum of the body will be:
solution:
Kinetic energy $(K)=\frac{P^2}{2 m}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{P}=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mK}}$
If $K_f=36 K_i$ So, $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}}=6 \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{i}}$ $\begin{aligned} \% \text { increase in momentum } & =\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{i}}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{i}}} \times 100 \% \\ & =\frac{6 \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{i}}-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{i}}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{i}}} \times 100 \% \\ & =500 \% \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A ball with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> collides with another identical ball at rest. After the collision, the direction of each ball makes an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the original direction. If the ratio of velocities of the balls after the collision is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>y</mi></math>, then what is the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>?
question: A ball with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> collides with another identical ball at rest. After the collision, the direction of each ball makes an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the original direction. If the ratio of velocities of the balls after the collision is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>y</mi></math>, then what is the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>?
solution:
From conservation of momentum along y-axis.

P→iy=P→fy

0+0=mv1sin30°j^+mv2sin30°-j^

mv2sin30°=mv1sin30°

v2=v1 or v1v2=1

q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : Body P having mass M moving with speed u has head-on collision elastically with another body Q having mass m initially at rest. If m≪M, body Q will have a maximum speed equal to 2u after collision.

Reason R : During elastic collision, the momentum and kinetic energy are both conserved.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : Body P having mass M moving with speed u has head-on collision elastically with another body Q having mass m initially at rest. If m≪M, body Q will have a maximum speed equal to 2u after collision.

Reason R : During elastic collision, the momentum and kinetic energy are both conserved.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: A is correct but R is not correct.
A is not correct but R is correct.
Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
solution:
For e=1 & second body at rest

V2=2m1u1 m1+m2=2uMM+m≃2u

Since M>>m

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> at rest explodes into three pieces, in the ratio of masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. Two smaller pieces fly off perpendicular to each other with velocities of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively. The velocity of the third piece will be
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> at rest explodes into three pieces, in the ratio of masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. Two smaller pieces fly off perpendicular to each other with velocities of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively. The velocity of the third piece will be
solution:
Let the masses of the three parts be m', m' and 2m'.

Momentum of first part p1=m'×30=30m' and momentum of second part p2=m'×40=40m'.

Third part will fly in a direction opposite to the direction of resultant of p1 and p2, so that total momentum of the system after explosion remains zero.

Momentum of the third part is p3=p12+p22=30m'2+40m'2=50m'

Velocity of third part, v=50m'2m'=25 m s-1

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A bullet of 10 g leaves the barrel of gun with a velocity of 600 m s-1. If the barrel of gun is 50 cm long and mass of gun is 3 kg, then value of impulse supplied to the gun will be:
question: A bullet of 10 g leaves the barrel of gun with a velocity of 600 m s-1. If the barrel of gun is 50 cm long and mass of gun is 3 kg, then value of impulse supplied to the gun will be:
solution:
The formula to calculate the impulse J on the gun is given by

J=mv-u   ...1

where, m is the mass, u is the initial velocity and v is the final velocity of the bullet.

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) to calculate the required impulse.

J=10 g×1 kg1000 g×600-0 m s-1= 6 N s

q: The position vector of the center of mass r→cm  of an asymmetric uniform bar of negligible area of cross-section as shown in figure is:
question: The position vector of the center of mass r→cm  of an asymmetric uniform bar of negligible area of cross-section as shown in figure is:
solution:
The position vector of center of mass r→cm is given as 

r→cm=Xcmx^+Ycm y^

Where, Xcm=x-coordinate of center of mass  and Ycm=y-coordinate of center of mass

r→cm=m1x1+m2x2+m3x3m1+m2+m3 x^ +m1y1+m2y2+m3y3m1+m2+m3 y^

r→cm=2m×L+m×2L+m5L22m+m+mx^+2m×L+m×L2+m×02m+m+my^

r→cm=13L8x^+5L8y^

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A machine gun of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> fires <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> bullets at the rate of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn></math> bullets per minute with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> each. The recoil velocity of the gun is :
question: A machine gun of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> fires <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> bullets at the rate of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn></math> bullets per minute with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> each. The recoil velocity of the gun is :
solution:
Number of bullets fired per second 18060=3.

Applying momentum conservation, we get

320×10-3×100=10v

⇒v=0.6 m s-1

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Three blocks A, B and C are lying on a smooth horizontal surface, as shown in the figure. A and B have equal masses, m while C has mass M. Block A is given an initial speed v towards B due to which it collides with B perfectly inelastically. The combined mass collides with C, also perfectly inelastically . 56th of the initial kinetic energy is lost in the whole process. What is the value of M/m?
question: Three blocks A, B and C are lying on a smooth horizontal surface, as shown in the figure. A and B have equal masses, m while C has mass M. Block A is given an initial speed v towards B due to which it collides with B perfectly inelastically. The combined mass collides with C, also perfectly inelastically . 56th of the initial kinetic energy is lost in the whole process. What is the value of M/m?
solution:
mv=m+mv′

m+mv′=m+m+Mvf

∴ final velocity vf=m2m+Mv

Initial energy =12mv2

Final energy  =122m+Mmv2m+M2

Given that 5612mv2=12mv2-122m+Mmv2m+M2

112mv2=12m2v22m+M

6m=2m+M

Mm=4

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Two blocks of masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> are placed on the same straight line with coordinates <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>cm</mi></math> respectively. The block of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is moved on the same line through a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> towards the other block. The distance through which the block of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> must be moved to keep the position of centre of mass of the system unchanged is
question: Two blocks of masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> are placed on the same straight line with coordinates <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>cm</mi></math> respectively. The block of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is moved on the same line through a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> towards the other block. The distance through which the block of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> must be moved to keep the position of centre of mass of the system unchanged is
solution:
Initial position of centre of mass: xCM=100+30×x40=3x4

Final position of the centre of mass: x'CM=106+30×x-y40=6+3x-y4

As position of centre of mass remains same,

xCM=x'CM ⇒3x4=6+3x-y4⇒3x=6+3x-3y  ⇒y=2 cm

Alternate method, ∆xCOM=m1∆x1+m2∆x2m1+m2⇒0=10×6+30y⇒y=-2 cm

q: A nucleus disintegrates into two nuclear parts, in such a way that ratio of their nuclear sizes is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><msup><mn>2</mn><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Their respective speed have a ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is_________
question: A nucleus disintegrates into two nuclear parts, in such a way that ratio of their nuclear sizes is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><msup><mn>2</mn><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Their respective speed have a ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is_________
solution:
Using the law of Momentum conservation

m1v1+m2(-v2)=0
⇒m1v1=m2v2
⇒m1m2=v2v1⇒4πρr3134πρr323=v2v1

It is given

 r1r2=1213
and r1r23=v2v1Therefore,12=v2v1
⇒v1v2=21

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A piece of wood of mass <math><mn>0.03</mn><mi> </mi><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></math> is dropped from the top of a <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi></math> height building. At the same time, a bullet of mass <math><mn>0.02</mn><mi> </mi><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></math> is fired vertically upward, with a velocity <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><msup><mrow><mi>s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> from the ground. The bullet gets embedded in the wood. Then the maximum height to which the combined system reaches above the top of the building before falling below is: <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><msup><mrow><mi>s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: A piece of wood of mass <math><mn>0.03</mn><mi> </mi><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></math> is dropped from the top of a <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi></math> height building. At the same time, a bullet of mass <math><mn>0.02</mn><mi> </mi><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></math> is fired vertically upward, with a velocity <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><msup><mrow><mi>s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> from the ground. The bullet gets embedded in the wood. Then the maximum height to which the combined system reaches above the top of the building before falling below is: <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><msup><mrow><mi>s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
Using relative velocity



arel=0

vrel=100

Time t for collision,

100=vrel×t

t=100100=1 sec

vbullet=100-1×10=90m/s

vparticle=10×1=10m/s

S=100×1-12×10×1=95 m



Pi=Pf

⇒90×0.02-10×0.03=0.05V

V=30m/s⇒h=v22g=90020=45 m

So from top of building

45-5=40 m

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Consider a circular disc of radius 20 cm with centre located at the origin. A circular hole of radius 5 cm is cut from this disc in such a way that the edge of the hole touches the edge of the disc. The distance of centre of mass of residual or remaining disc from the origin will be
question: Consider a circular disc of radius 20 cm with centre located at the origin. A circular hole of radius 5 cm is cut from this disc in such a way that the edge of the hole touches the edge of the disc. The distance of centre of mass of residual or remaining disc from the origin will be
solution: $\text { mass of disc }=\mathrm{m}$ $\text { mass of cut part }=\frac{m}{16}$ $X_{c o m}=\frac{m \times 0-\frac{m}{16} \times 15}{m-\frac{m}{16}}$ $=1 \mathrm{~cm} .$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q:
In two different experiments, an object of mass 5 kg moving with a speed of 25 ms-1 hits two different walls and comes to rest within
(i) 3 second, (ii) 5 seconds, respectively.
Choose the correct option out of the following :
question:
In two different experiments, an object of mass 5 kg moving with a speed of 25 ms-1 hits two different walls and comes to rest within
(i) 3 second, (ii) 5 seconds, respectively.
Choose the correct option out of the following :
options: Impulse and average force acting on the object will be same for both the cases.
Impulse will be same for both the cases but the average force will be different.
Average force will be same for both the cases but the impulse will be different.
Average force and impulse will be different for both the cases.
solution:
Impulse = Change in momentum

I=ΔP

Therefore, in both the cases the impulse is same.

Average force is defined as,

Favg =ΔPΔt

Since the time in the two cases is different, the average force will be different.

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: The identical spheres each of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>M</mi></math> are placed at the corners of a right angled triangle with mutually perpendicular sides equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> each. Taking point of intersection of these two sides as origin, the magnitude of position vector of the centre of mass of the system is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>4</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mrow><mi>x</mi></mfrac></math>, where the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ________.
question: The identical spheres each of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>M</mi></math> are placed at the corners of a right angled triangle with mutually perpendicular sides equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> each. Taking point of intersection of these two sides as origin, the magnitude of position vector of the centre of mass of the system is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>4</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mrow><mi>x</mi></mfrac></math>, where the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ________.
solution:
Position vector r→COM=m1r→1+m2r→2+m3r→3 m1+m2+m3

r→COM =2M×0+2M×4i^+2M×4j^6M

⇒r→=43i^+43j^

|r→|=432+432=423

Therefore, x=3.

q: A rubber ball is released from a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> above the floor. It bounces back repeatedly, always rising to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>81</mn><mn>100</mn></mfrac></math> of the height through which it falls. Find the average speed of the ball. (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A rubber ball is released from a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> above the floor. It bounces back repeatedly, always rising to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>81</mn><mn>100</mn></mfrac></math> of the height through which it falls. Find the average speed of the ball. (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
v0=2gh

v=e2gh=2gh

⇒e=0.9

S=h+2e2h+2e4h+………

t=2h g+2e2h g+2e22h g+………

vav=st=2.5 m s-1

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: The figure shows the position - time $(x-t)$ graph of one-dimensional motion of a body of mass $0.4 \mathrm{~kg}$. The magnitude of each impulse is
question: The figure shows the position - time $(x-t)$ graph of one-dimensional motion of a body of mass $0.4 \mathrm{~kg}$. The magnitude of each impulse is
solution:
From the graph, it is a straight line so, uniform motion. Because of impulse direction of velocity changes as can be seen from the slope of the graph.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Initial velocity }=\frac{2}{2}=1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\
& \text { Final velocity }=-\frac{2}{2}=-1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\
& \qquad \bar{P}_{\mathrm{i}}=0.4 \mathrm{~N}-\mathrm{s} \\
& \qquad \overline{\mathrm{P}}_{\mathrm{i}}=-0.4 \mathrm{~N}-\mathrm{s} \\
& \overrightarrow{\mathrm{J}}=\overline{\mathrm{P}}_{\mathrm{i}}-\overline{\mathrm{P}}_{\mathrm{i}}=-0.4-0.4=-0.8 \mathrm{~N}-\mathrm{s}(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{J}}=\text { impulse }) \\
& |\overrightarrow{\mathrm{J}}|=0.8 \mathrm{~N}-\mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A ball is dropped from a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. If the coefficient of restitution for the collision between ball and floor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math>, after hitting the floor, the ball rebounds to a height of ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
question: A ball is dropped from a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. If the coefficient of restitution for the collision between ball and floor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math>, after hitting the floor, the ball rebounds to a height of ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
solution:
As we know h'=e2h, where h'=height after the collision, h=height before the collision and e=coefficient of restitution.

Therefore,

h'=0.52×20 m=5 m

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A man (mass <math><mo>=</mo><mn>50</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> ) and his son (mass <math><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> ) are standing on a frictionless surface facing each other. The man pushes his son so that he starts moving at a speed of <math><mn>0.70</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> with respect to the man. The speed of the man with respect to the surface is:
question: A man (mass <math><mo>=</mo><mn>50</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> ) and his son (mass <math><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> ) are standing on a frictionless surface facing each other. The man pushes his son so that he starts moving at a speed of <math><mn>0.70</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> with respect to the man. The speed of the man with respect to the surface is:
solution:
By conservation of linear momentum,

P→i=P→f

Where P→i and P→f are initial and final momentum of system (boy + man)

Now,  0=mbv→b+mmv→m......(i)

vb= velocity of boy w.r.t. ground

vm= velocity of man w.r.t. ground

v→bm= velocity of boy w.r.t. man

So, v→bm=v→b-v→m

or v→b=v→bm+v→m

or v→b=0.7+v→m ........(ii)

From equation (i) and (ii),

0=200.7+v→m+50 v→m

v→m=-1470=-0.20 m s-1

Negative sign shows velocity of man is opposite to boy.

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: The block of mass $M$ moving on the frictionless horizontal surface collides with a spring of spring constant $\mathrm{K}$ and compresses it by length $\mathrm{L}$. The maximum momentum of the block after collision is
question: The block of mass $M$ moving on the frictionless horizontal surface collides with a spring of spring constant $\mathrm{K}$ and compresses it by length $\mathrm{L}$. The maximum momentum of the block after collision is
solution: $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{KL}^2=\frac{\mathrm{P}^2}{2 \mathrm{~m}} \quad \therefore \mathrm{P}=\sqrt{\mathrm{MK}} \mathrm{L}$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A rod of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is lying on a horizontal frictionless surface. A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> travelling along the surface hits at one end of the rod with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> in a direction perpendicular to the rod. The collision is completely elastic. After collision, particle comes to rest. The ratio of masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi>m</mi><mi>M</mi></mfrac></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be
question: A rod of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is lying on a horizontal frictionless surface. A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> travelling along the surface hits at one end of the rod with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> in a direction perpendicular to the rod. The collision is completely elastic. After collision, particle comes to rest. The ratio of masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi>m</mi><mi>M</mi></mfrac></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be
solution:
From momentum conservation, Pi0=Pf
mu=Mv …i
From angular momentum conservation about O,
mu·L2=ML212ω
⇒ω=6muML …(ii)
From e=R.V·SR.V·A
1=V+ωL2u

V+ωL2=u
V+3muM=u
muM+3muM=u
4muM=u
mM=14
x=4

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: The centre of mass of a solid hemisphere of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the centre of the flat surface. Then value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is
question: The centre of mass of a solid hemisphere of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the centre of the flat surface. Then value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is
solution:
Centre of Mass of a solid hemisphere from centre of the  flat surface is given by,hcm=3R8

It is given that R= 8 cm

Therefore,

 x=hcm=3R8=3×88x=3 cm
